name: Castilian

ancestors.notes: Hispanic Vulgar Latin: primary descent. Castilian emerged in the eastern Cantabrian foothills (~9th c. CE) as the vernacular of the County of Castile, then expanded rapidly southward with the Reconquista.
Celtiberian substrate: the Celtic-speaking Arevaci, Pelendones, and Celtiberi of the central meseta. Likely source of some phonotactic patterns and vocabulary.
Basque/Aquitanian substrate: strong in northern Castile (La Rioja, Burgos, Álava). The F→H→∅ change (hijo < FILIU) is widely attributed to Basque substrate influence, as Basque lacks /f/ natively.
Visigothic superstrate: the Visigoths ruled Hispania 418–711 CE. ~300 Germanic loanwords (guerra, guardar, robar, blanco, orgullo); possible influence on stress patterns.

Andalusi Arabic: the most significant lexical adstrate in Castilian, with ~4,000 Arabic loanwords — the highest density of any European language. Includes: algebra, alcohol, aceite, almohada, azúcar, cifra, ojalá, hasta. Phonological influence on the uvular/velar fricative /x/ (jota) is debated.
Mozarabic: the Ibero-Romance of the Christian communities in al-Andalus. Absorbed into Castilian through the Reconquista resettlement of the meseta and Andalusia.

notes: Old Spanish phonology (c. 1200–1500), as attested in the Cantar de mio Cid (c. 1140–1207) and contemporaneous texts. The system is defined by a six-sibilant opposition (three voicing pairs): affricate /ts/~/dz/ (written ⟨c/ç⟩ vs ⟨z⟩), lamino-alveolar fricative /s/~/z/ (written ⟨ss⟩ vs ⟨s⟩ intervocalically), and palato-alveolar fricative /ʃ/~/ʒ/ (written ⟨x⟩ vs ⟨j, g+e,i⟩). All six sibilants collapsed into the modern two-way /s/~/θ/ opposition by c. 1600 through a series of mergers (devoicing, affricate simplification, retraction). Additional period features: ⟨ll⟩ = /ʎ/ (yeísmo post-medieval); ⟨v⟩ = /v~β/ distinct from ⟨b⟩ = /b/ (betacism complete by 16th c.); intervocalic ⟨s⟩ = /z/ vs ⟨ss⟩ = /s/; no /θ/ or /x/ phonemes. Source: Penny (2002), §§3.1–3.3; Lapesa (1981), §§52–56.

sources.author: Penny, R.
sources.title: A History of the Spanish Language
sources.notes: Primary reference for the Old Spanish six-sibilant system and its 16th-century merger into modern Spanish.

sources.author: Lapesa, R.
sources.title: Historia de la lengua española (9th ed.)
sources.notes: Standard history of the Spanish language; covers the Old Spanish sibilant system and its evolution.
